danger_signals.visual_form: Battle scene initiation with color shift and sprite appearance
danger_signals.meaning: Proximity to hostile forces or trapped area

danger_signals.visual_form: Character sprite flickering or altered posture
danger_signals.meaning: Danger present or threat imminent in area

danger_signals.visual_form: Spike tiles, lava, falling debris depicted in tileset
danger_signals.meaning: Immediate physical danger to player character

danger_signals.visual_form: Palette shifts toward darker/desaturated colors in dungeon areas
danger_signals.meaning: Increased monster density or cursed/dangerous location

safety_signals.visual_form: Warm-toned buildings, NPCs standing idle without animation
safety_signals.meaning: Safe zone with healing and commerce available

safety_signals.visual_form: Interior room graphics with furniture and peaceful ambiance
safety_signals.meaning: Designated safe space for recovery and trade

safety_signals.visual_form: Character sprite facing player with speech bubble indicator
safety_signals.meaning: Information, quests, or safe interaction available

safety_signals.visual_form: Pastoral tileset with minimal enemy sprites visible

faction_indicators.color_identity: Varied per character; typically bright sprite palettes
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Treasure Hunter emblem (implied by game title)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Party grouping in overworld; distinctive character sprites in towns

faction_indicators.color_identity: Darker or contrasting palettes relative to party
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Different sprite designs per monster type and rarity
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Cluster placement in dungeons and wilderness; battle initiation indicates faction territoriality

faction_indicators.color_identity: Varied civilian sprites; earth tones and neutral hues
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Fixed positioning in towns and villages; dialogue triggers indicate allegiance or neutrality

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Bright overworld palette, accessible villages, minimal dungeon presence
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: NPCs move freely; enemies appear in designated areas only; dialogue is expository

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Darker palette, hazard tiles visible, enemy sprites populate terrain
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Random encounters enabled; NPCs absent; visual cues for traps and obstacles

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Return to bright outdoor palette; reduced enemy encounter frequency
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemies may not respawn immediately; NPCs acknowledge quest completion through altered dialogue states

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Dramatic scene transition; distinctive boss sprite design and scale
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Battle music shift; unavoidable encounter; arena-like spatial setup

environmental_storytelling.example: Dungeons show progressive decay (crumbling walls, darkened floors) indicating age, curse, or neglect
Characters clustered in town squares or quest-giving locations signal social structures and power centers
Valuable items surrounded by high-encounter-rate terrain or boss monsters indicates danger-to-reward ratios
Impassable terrain or locked doors visualized through tileset barriers communicate quest progression gating
Specific enemy sprites confined to thematic areas (e.g., undead in graveyards, fire creatures in lava zones) reinforce world coherence

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Standard walk animation; facing direction responsive to input; interaction prompt appears near NPCs
Movement may trigger random battle transitions; sprite may show damage/status effects through animation
No sprite movement or minimal animation; presence indicated only by tile occupancy
Sprite animation activates; attack patterns visualized through attack animations and screen effects
Sprite remains static facing player; dialogue box appears with distinctive text speed/sound effects
Dialogue text changes; sprite may show gratitude animation or relaxed posture

notes: Treasure Hunter G (1996, SNES) uses traditional RPG visual language with palette-based faction/danger communication. Without access to gameplay footage or detailed design documentation, the above represents inferred patterns from SNES-era RPG conventions. The game employs standard tileset variation for environmental storytelling and sprite-based NPC behavior to convey world state transitions. Further empirical observation of actual gameplay would refine these descriptors.
